cards.front: How do you enter interface configuration mode for a single port versus a range?
cards.back: Single: `interface gigabitethernet0/1` (abbrev `int gi0/1` or `int g0/1`). Range: `interface range gi0/1 - 5` or `interface range gi0/1, gi0/3, gi0/5`. Same commands then apply to every port in the range.

cards.front: How do you set port speed and duplex manually, and what are the defaults?
cards.back: `speed {10|100|1000|auto}` and `duplex {half|full|auto}`. Default is `auto` for both — IEEE autonegotiation. If both ends auto: matched. If one is fixed and the other auto: mismatch risk (auto side often falls back to half-duplex on a 100 Mbps link).

cards.front: What does `show interfaces status` reveal compared to `show ip interface brief`?
cards.back: `show interfaces status` (switch-only) gives one line per port: status, VLAN, duplex, speed, type. `show ip interface brief` gives interface, IP address, OK?, status, protocol — usable on routers and switches. Use `status` for switch port quick view; `brief` for IP/L3 view.

cards.front: What does the 'line protocol is up/down' field really mean on `show interfaces`?
cards.back: Two-part status: 'Interface' = layer 1 (cable/medium); 'line protocol' = layer 2 (keepalives, framing, encapsulation). 'up/up' = working. 'up/down' = L2 issue (encapsulation, keepalive, no neighbor). 'down/down' = L1 issue (cable, no signal). 'administratively down' = `shutdown` set.

cards.front: What are runts, giants, CRC errors, and late collisions in `show interfaces` output?
cards.back: Runts: frames < 64 bytes (often collisions or bad NIC). Giants: > 1518 bytes (or 1522 with 802.1Q) — usually MTU mismatch. CRC errors: FCS check failed (cable, EMI, duplex mismatch). Late collisions: collision after first 512 bits — almost always indicates a duplex mismatch.

cards.front: If you see 'CRC' and 'late collisions' on a switch interface, what's the most likely cause?
cards.back: Duplex mismatch — one side full-duplex, other side half-duplex. Half-duplex side detects collisions late because the full-duplex side ignores CSMA/CD and transmits whenever. Verify with `show interfaces` and `show interfaces … status`; fix by hard-coding both ends to full duplex or letting both autonegotiate.

cards.front: How do you administratively disable an interface and re-enable it?
cards.back: Disable: `shutdown` in interface config. Re-enable: `no shutdown`. Disabled state shows as 'administratively down' in `show ip interface brief`.

cards.front: Why is it best practice to manually disable autonegotiation on switch-to-switch trunks/uplinks but leave it on access ports?
cards.back: Trunks: hard-code speed/duplex (gigabit/full) to avoid autoneg quirks during convergence and for predictable performance. Access ports: leave auto-MDIX and autoneg on so any user device works without manual config.
